topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: What is the federal third-party doctrine?
back: A person has no reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily turned over to a third party, so the government may obtain it without a warrant.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Which two SCOTUS cases are the 'twin pillars' of the third-party doctrine?
back: Smith v. Maryland (pen-register dialing) and United States v. Miller (bank records).

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Smith v. Maryland, what did the Court hold about a pen register recording dialed phone numbers?
back: Using a pen register to record dialed numbers is not a search; there is no reasonable expectation of privacy in numbers voluntarily conveyed to the phone company.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Smith v. Maryland: 'This Court consistently has held that a person has no legitimate expectation of privacy in information he ___ to third parties.'
back: voluntarily turns over

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Which SCOTUS case held that police use of a pen register to record the numbers dialed from a phone is not a search?
back: Smith v. Maryland (1979) — the origin of the third-party doctrine.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Why does Smith v. Maryland matter to the third-party doctrine?
back: It is the origin/articulation of the doctrine — the case that established no REP exists in information voluntarily conveyed to a third party.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In United States v. Miller, what did the Court hold about a bank customer's records?
back: Bank records are the bank's business records, not the customer's private papers; there is no reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily conveyed to and exposed to the bank.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Which SCOTUS case held that a customer has no reasonable expectation of privacy in checks and deposit slips held by his bank?
back: United States v. Miller (1976) — a foundational third-party-doctrine case (pre-Smith).

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Carpenter v. United States, what did the Court hold about government acquisition of historical cell-site location information (CSLI)?
back: Acquiring historical CSLI is a Fourth Amendment search that requires a warrant.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Per Carpenter, does the third-party doctrine reach the comprehensive, auto-generated record of movements that historical CSLI provides?
back: No — Carpenter held the third-party doctrine does NOT extend to comprehensive, automatically generated location records like CSLI.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Did Carpenter v. United States overrule Smith v. Maryland or United States v. Miller?
back: No. Carpenter is narrow — it did not disturb Smith/Miller, conventional surveillance, or ordinary business records; Smith and Miller remain good law.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Carpenter described CSLI location records as ones that 'hold for many Americans the "___."'
back: privacies of life

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Are the 'Carpenter prongs' (new category of info; generated without meaningful voluntary choice; reveals the privacies of life) a holding the Court enumerated?
back: No. The three prongs are the instructor's interpretive close-reading gloss, NOT a test the Court enumerated. Only the phrase 'the privacies of life' is the Court's own. Never present the prongs as a holding.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: As an interpretive gloss (not a holding), what is the first 'prong' the instructor uses to summarize Carpenter?
back: A new category of information arising from the digital age — instructor framing, not language the Court enumerated.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: As an interpretive gloss (not a holding), what is the second 'prong' the instructor uses to summarize Carpenter?
back: Information generated without meaningful voluntary choice — instructor framing, not language the Court enumerated.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: As an interpretive gloss (not a holding), what is the third 'prong' the instructor uses to summarize Carpenter — and which part is actually the Court's words?
back: Information that reveals 'the privacies of life.' Only the phrase 'the privacies of life' is the Court's own language; the three-prong framing itself is interpretive gloss.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Kyllo v. United States, what did the Court hold about thermal imaging of a home?
back: Using a thermal imager (sense-enhancing technology not in general public use) to detect interior details that would otherwise require physical intrusion is a search, presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Kyllo: 'Where, as here, the Government uses a device that is ___, to explore details of the home that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion, the surveillance is a "search"...'
back: not in general public use

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: What separate line does Kyllo draw for surveillance technology?
back: Sense-enhancing technology 'not in general public use' used to expose the interior of a home is a search.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: What is the status of applying Carpenter to newer surveillance tech (pole cameras, license-plate readers, drones)?
back: Unsettled — there is no settled federal rule; lower courts divide. It is the open frontier of the doctrine.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: What is the scale/mosaic dividing line for evaluating new surveillance tech under Carpenter?
back: A few fixed cameras capturing public movement tend to stay on the Smith/Knotts (not-a-search) side; comprehensive, city-wide, persistent tracking pulls toward Carpenter (warrant required).

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In United States v. Hay (10th Cir. 2024), what did the court hold about ~2 months of pole-camera surveillance of a home's public-view exterior?
back: It was not a search — the camera captured only what was visible to passersby from a public vantage, and the court held Carpenter did not abrogate its prior pole-camera precedent.
cite: United States v. Hay, 95 F.4th 1304 (10th Cir. 2024)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: How should United States v. Hay's pole-camera ruling be treated as authority?
back: As one circuit's persuasive view, not a settled federal rule — pole-camera law is split and unsettled; circuits and state high courts divide on whether long-term pole-camera surveillance of a home is a search.
cite: United States v. Hay, 95 F.4th 1304 (10th Cir. 2024)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In United States v. Porter (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026), what did the court hold about use of an automatic license-plate reader (ALPR)?
back: Use of an ALPR for vehicle-location data was not a search, and the ALPR hit supplied reasonable suspicion for the stop. (Circuit — persuasive.)
cite: United States v. Porter, No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: How must United States v. Porter (the ALPR case) be cited, and what cite must you avoid?
back: Cite by docket: No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026). It is too recent for a confirmed F.4th reporter cite — never assert a reporter cite for it.
cite: United States v. Porter, No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026) (state — illustrative, non-binding), what did the court hold about a Flock ALPR camera network?
back: A 172-camera Flock ALPR network was not a search — no REP in a vehicle's public movements, and it was not the 'near perfect surveillance' that triggered a warrant in Carpenter. (State — illustrative, non-binding.)
cite: Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: How must Robinson v. Commonwealth (the Flock ALPR case) be cited and weighted?
back: Cite by court and date only — Va. Ct. App., Apr. 7, 2026 — as state, illustrative, non-binding. It is too recent for a confirmed S.E.2d reporter cite; never assert a reporter cite or a 2024 date for it.
cite: Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle v. Baltimore Police Dep't (4th Cir. 2021, en banc), what did the court hold about a city-wide aerial wide-area surveillance program?
back: The program tracking everyone's public movements required a warrant under Carpenter — the scale/mosaic counterpoint to the ALPR cases.
cite: Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle v. Baltimore Police Dep't, 2 F.4th 330 (4th Cir. 2021) (en banc)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Contrast the ALPR cases (Porter, Robinson) with Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle on the scale/mosaic line.
back: The ALPR cases (limited camera capture of public movement) found no search; Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle (comprehensive city-wide aerial tracking) required a warrant — scale/persistence is the dividing line.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Is there a Supreme Court case directly governing police drone surveillance?
back: No — there is no SCOTUS drone decision. Drone law is emerging, jurisdiction-dependent, and unsettled.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Which SCOTUS aerial-surveillance cases are the analogues for drones — and why might they not fit?
back: The manned-aircraft lineage — Ciraolo, Riley, and Dow Chemical — is the bridge, but drones may not fit: they fly below navigable airspace, can hover/persist/zoom, and are cheap and pervasive, pulling toward Kyllo and Carpenter.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In California v. Ciraolo, what did the Court hold about naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced backyard from ~1,000 ft?
back: It is not a search — the curtilage was knowingly exposed to any aircraft lawfully in public navigable airspace.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Florida v. Riley, what did the plurality hold about naked-eye helicopter observation into curtilage at 400 ft?
back: It was not a search (plurality), where the aircraft was in lawful, non-rare public airspace — the closest SCOTUS analogue for low-altitude drones.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: In Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, what did the Court hold about EPA aerial photography of an industrial complex with a commonly available camera?
back: It was not a search; sense-enhancement by widely available technology over open commercial areas did not cross the line — the contrast to Kyllo's 'not in general public use.'
cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986)

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Distinguish Kyllo from Dow Chemical on the surveillance-technology line.
back: Kyllo: tech 'not in general public use' exposing a home's interior is a search. Dow Chemical: a commonly available aerial camera over commercial premises is not a search.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: What is the pitfall of over-reading Carpenter?
back: Treating it as a general repeal of the third-party doctrine. It is narrow — Smith and Miller remain good law, and it does not disturb conventional surveillance or ordinary business records.

topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
front: Why is it a pitfall to treat Ciraolo/Riley as settled drone law?
back: They predate drones and Carpenter; low, persistent, zoom-capable drones may not fit them cleanly. Drone law remains emerging and unsettled.
